speaker: interviewer
text: In the interview with Mr. Stefan Stok. At the end of last tape, you were telling me about the changing in the life of the ghettos-- rumors, and changing, and if you can continue telling about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So there were signs, which were not good. And those signs were probably increasing. And there were a number of times that there were executions of hostages, or people who were arrested. And, indeed, in the-- in mid-July, those things were-- there were more signs. And when I came, there was an execution of over 100 people, 110 people I think. And some of them were Jewish police, which was just like that, without any reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, yes. But that was, again, they were taken by name. And they were executed. And that must have been around maybe in the second half of July. And, suddenly, the atmosphere started getting very panicky. When I came home from school, or from sitting in this garden, I think on the 19th of July, I saw a car, a Gestapo car, somewhere near [POLISH]. And we found out that they were arresting all kinds of people, like doctors, and lawyers, and so on. And my father even had-- I think we were discussing if he should stay or hide. And we-- and there were as well things, like in the house next door there was living a man who was a cousin, or a nephew, of the [INAUDIBLE] who was one of those ghetto barons, who had very close connections with the Germans. And his name was [INAUDIBLE] as well. And he said to some people he knew in our house that things are very bad, because there is a new unit which came to Warsaw, a German unit. And they called it, I think, [GERMAN], or something, [GERMAN]. We just heard those words. But we, of course, didn't know what it meant. And that, they are going to start some activity against the ghetto. And, of course, [GERMAN] was-- which we didn't know-- came from the name of [GERMAN], who was the-- who was the protector the German-- protector of in Czechoslovakia, in Prague. And he was basically a deputy of Himmler. And he was active in anti-Jewish acts, sins, before the war even. And he was assassinated. He was killed by British agents. And in his honor, so to say, they planned the action of extermination in [POLISH], all over Poland. And there was really a group unit, which came to Warsaw in July, '42. And they started the deportation action, which we called [POLISH]. We called it action. And it started, really, on the 22nd of July, '42. And it started immediately by-- I mean, there were already 19th, 20th, 21, everything, it was obvious that something was going to happen. On 22nd, I think, everywhere the posters were put, everywhere, about [GERMAN], about transportation. [GERMAN] is not exactly transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's how to say, something transporting you from-- resettlement. And where are you going to be resettled? In the east. Who is going to be resettled? Everybody, except for certain categories. In other words, all of the population of the ghetto, except for certain categories. But the categories, there were lots of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were all the [GERMAN], and all of their families, the Jewish police and their families, the doctors and the workers in the hospital and their families, the workers in the shops working for the German industry, or for the German army and their families, [INAUDIBLE] and their families, and so on, and so forth. So there were quite a lot of categories of people who were to be spared from the [GERMAN]. As for the rest, they had to appear with their things, with a small amount of things because they couldn't take so much, and with some basic requirements, and clothes, and so on, on the [GERMAN], which was the kind of train siding in the north end of the ghetto. And from there, they are going to be transported to the east. And those who were going to come their voluntarily, at least in the early stages, they were even going to have some food given to them, I think three kilos of bread and a kilo of marmalade, something like that. So there were a number of people who went there who were hungry, and who went voluntarily. And that was all. Those posters appeared on the 22nd, I think. And on the same day, or the day before I'm not sure, [GERMAN], who was the head of the [GERMAN], committed suicide. So when [GERMAN] committed suicide, everybody knew that it was-- the whole thing was very bad. And [GERMAN] committed suicide because he was forced, he was told, to sign those notices. And he refused. He refused. And in the end, he didn't sign. But he committed suicide straight after. It was possibly a mistake that he didn't speak openly to the people. But that was-- but, anyway, he showed a great amount of courage by committing suicide as well. And we, we must have already been warned greatly the day, or two, or three days before, because before the posters appeared, already on the day before, on the 21st I think, we went to the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what does it mean? It's the shop, as I said, where factories which were working for the British-- working for the German army, or for the German industry. And there were quite a number of them in the ghetto. To But the real big ones were [POLISH], and [POLISH], and there were some [POLISH]. There were some others. There were probably quite a number of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my uncles, brothers of my mother [POLISH], they all-- those are nicknames, really. But that's how I always knew them. And [POLISH], they were all working in textiles already before the war. And they had their little factories, small factories, probably right from the beginning. I was not very much aware of them. But they existed right from the beginning of the war. And those factories, like other workshops, were incorporated in a really big industrial concern. And a German industrialist was put as a head of this concern. It was [INAUDIBLE]. And in our case, it was H. Schultz, and as I said, some others. And the Schultz factory, where the factories of my uncles, were incorporated, was in [POLISH] Street. Already before the war, it must have been a big industrial place. There was a big, red brick buildings, maybe five stories high, on quite a large area. And, of course, once the announcement about the action was there, people tried to get, somehow, one way or another, to get the magic card that they are somehow saved from the [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] is the resettlement. And everybody tried. But it was terribly difficult, of course. And if they could get into a place like the shop, they would of course take places work in the shop. But working in the shop, there were, of course, workers who were working in the shop before. There were workers who were busy in those factories all through the ghetto. But they were very poorly paid, or not paid at all, and having some kind of a poor food rations given to them. And they had to work all day for very poor food rations. So people did it only out of complete necessity. But now, suddenly, as it was a ticket to survive, the ticket to be exempt from the [GERMAN], suddenly there was a rush to get into the shops. And we were so unbelievably lucky from that point of view, because those were my uncles, they put the family in the shop. Otherwise, it was terribly difficult. You had to pay money. And even then, it was lots of money. And probably even then, it was not at all certain, because after all that you could get into a shop, and get an official identity, official card, official exemption, because after all those shops had a limited number of places. And now, suddenly, probably 10 times as many people as there was place for wanted all to get permission to get in. But already on the 21st of July, I think, we went to the shop, because we didn't stay in our house. And I remember that there was the atmosphere, even on this first day when nobody yet was properly deported, or very few people were deported. But the whole place was like in a state of complete panic. Everywhere, people were everywhere. And people were everywhere [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] frightened, terribly worried, terribly upset. And we walked through those streets. And in front of the Schultz shop in [POLISH] Street, there was, again, masses of people who were trying to break the door into the shop. And there was some strong men standing kind of-- there was a shop who was called [? Singer, ?] who was a German Jew, according to what I knew. And they were pushing people inside, and beating people. It was really, absolutely horrendous, everything that was happening there. And those crowds and crowds of people who were trying to get into the shop, just like that, and being beaten, and being pushed, and being treated absolutely horribly. And because, of course, we were officially permitted, somehow we managed to get into it. And we went. We got into the shop. And that, I think, is probably the last I saw our flat in [POLISH]. I don't think we ever went back. I think my parents probably managed to get some bits of furniture from there, and some things out of there. And we still-- the old sofa was still moving with us during that. My mother somehow still managed to get a cart, or some kind of transport, to get that sofa into one of the places we moved on. But otherwise, I never went back there. And I forgot to tell you about the bridge, but it doesn't matter. And then we spent the next weeks, we spent very close in the shop, all the time in the shop, or shop I mean a factory. It was a factory building, or near to the factory building. And we never dared to move, certainly not during those weeks after, to move far from there, because the weeks after-- the weeks, that means between the end of July 20th of July and something like the 7th, 8th of September, that was the so-called big action, where practically the whole of the ghetto was emptied, when people were taken, and brought to the [GERMAN], brought to the siding, taken from there in trains, and shipped to Treblinka. We didn't know what Treblinka was at this stage. And we didn't know that it existed. And we didn't know what was happening with the people. But the fear was already absolutely immense. And I don't think many people had a good-- had any very hopeful feeling about what was-- I think there were people who were deluding themselves, for sure, and people who thought it was quite inconceivable that the end was going to be death. But I think there was a general people tried to avoid being deported as much as they could. What happened then was the ghetto get already on the beginning of the action, ghetto was immediately surrounded by Polish police, and even more by German auxiliary battalions of Ukrainians, and of the Lats, and of the Lithuanians. And those Lithuanians, and Lats, and Ukrainians, together with the SS of course, and the Jewish police, they were then responsible for getting people to the [GERMAN]. And they did it in the way that they were closing areas of the ghetto. They were closing blocks of houses, or streets, or part of the street, and telling everybody to come down, and then taking them, with few exceptions, to the [GERMAN], and putting them on the trains. And it was all done with a tremendous amount of-- particularly then in this summer-- with a tremendous amount of brutality, in the most brutal and horrible way, which was all purpose done, because the idea was, I suppose, that if one terrorizes the people enough, then nobody is going to be able to offer any resistance, or even to refuse, or even to run away. And it worked. It worked in so far--

words.text: was not very much aware of

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was it brutal?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe in what way you remember it was brutal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think when I come to my particular experience, maybe. But already then, in summer, the brutality was just amazing. It was-- brutality was simply by-- first of all, everything was going to-- was doing with screaming and shouting. There was never any normal talking. There was screaming. There was screaming for people to come down. There was screaming for people to assemble. There was shouting. Then people were all the time being kept, somehow terrorized. And then there was everything. Once they were kept waiting-- but once they started moving, everything had to be done on the double. They had to run rather than walk. Or they had to walk very fast. And people were beaten at the same time so that they would not even think about-- so there was a conscious policy of terror. Some people were shot in front of others, and maybe children were killed in front of others so that there would be such policy of fear and terror that nobody would be even able to think of offering resistance. And I think that fear was everything. I think that the fear was there during the action, even if you were hiding in the shop. Even if there was not immediate danger at this moment, there was a real-- I mean, something that was not experienced in the ghetto beforehand, to such an extent. There was terrible deprivation. And there was terrible poverty, and so on. But the terror that was experienced now was not experienced before. And I was a child. So I was not-- so the shop was, as I said, a German managed factory, a big factory with halls and halls full of machines. And this area where we were, that area was called [GERMAN]. That was a knitting factory, where they were doing things like socks, and gloves, and knitted wear, probably jumpers, and so on. So there were big halls full of machines, full of workers, and so on. And my father was then started working. And my mother as well, they were working in this big room. But we-- that means my cousins who were about the same age as me and I-- we, of course, had no right to be there anyway, not official. So we were-- whenever we heard the Germans came into the shop, that anybody like that came into the shop, we were hiding. We were disappearing. We were hiding somewhere behind the furniture. We were hiding-- what I remember exactly was there were big shelves along the sides of the rooms, with big bales of material. And we would somehow crawl and hide behind those bales of material, and spend quite a long time there, until the Germans would leave the shop. It could have been-- quite often, maybe it was a false alarm, because sometimes there would be maybe somebody only coming to check something with the management. But they made, as well, searches inside the shop. And then the actions was, and the blockades in this time in July, August, where they were sudden. They were coming completely suddenly. And although the area of the factory-- that means not only the factory building, but as well an area of maybe some hundreds of meters around, was all separated now from the rest of the ghetto. It was enclosed with barbed wire, and gates with barbed wire. And it was, so to say, a separate area, which was, now that I think about it, it was almost already a camp, in a way, separated from the rest of the ghetto. And it was, in a way as well, the blockades shouldn't have happened there, because it was part of the factory working for the Wehrmacht, and so on. All the same, the blockades happened. And the blockades, some of them were absolutely horrendous. Some of them were going on for days, for two, three days, when we never dreamt of moving out of the hiding place when we had a hiding place there inside the shop. And we had-- but pretty soon, we were, as well, we found some apartment on the Schultz terrain, but not in the shop itself, but there was the one that we moved into was, I think, in [POLISH] 49. I think it was opposite, roughly. It was very near. And it was a big flat on the maybe third floor. I think it was on the third floor. And the owners of the flat were not there. The flat was empty. All the furniture was there. All the things were there. All the stuff was still there. But the owners must have already gone to Treblinka in one of the early transports. But if we thought that there is-- I think we spent some nights in this flat. But I think that there was-- because my uncles, as I said, were department managers, they had some prior information from Schultz, from the factory owner, if something worrying was going to happen nearby or not. And I think when there was a great worry, we would not go even out of the shop. And when they thought that things were a bit peaceful, we would maybe go and spend a day or a night in this apartment. But even Schultz probably didn't know everything. And even because although he was German, and although he was director of the big factory which was employing 10 or 12,000 people, the SS didn't give him all the information. Sometimes he would get some information. And sometimes he wouldn't. And all this period of July August was a period of total uncertainty and total panic. And there were many blockades. And later towards the end of August, the blockades started taking part in the Schultz shop as well. And then they were suddenly reducing the number of the workers. There was a blockade of so-called green numbers, where everybody had to have green number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would give you a little green ticket with a number.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not everybody, if they would have part of the shop, like [INAUDIBLE], when they officially had maybe 2,000 workers, they would have numbers only for 800, not talking about the illegal ones, because we were illegal. Right, I was illegal. Children were not counted. Only those who worked there were counted. So maybe they would give you for less than half. And so then there was a selection. And then my father went for the selection, because he had a green number. But my mother didn't have a green number. So she already was hiding together with me. And I, of course, didn't. So we were hiding in some high up in the attics of the factory, in some place where you had to go through three little hidden doors, and so on. And then, finally, you were up in the attic. And there, we were hiding, and really shaking out of our mind. And it went on for hours and for hours. And then the Ukrainians, or whoever it was, were searching. But somehow, we managed to survive. But even those who-- first of all, those who went, even those who didn't have the numbers, and went to the selection, of course were sent left, and sent in the direction of [POLISH]. And even some those with the green numbers were suddenly selected out. I mean, it was not-- SS did what it wanted. Nobody-- there was no-- they might have given you some kind of promise. But there was no reason why they should have kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is the end of tape number six.
